coredata.dc:title: New luminescent solar concentrator windows using PMMA-InP/ZnS Nanohybrid coating films for green building applications
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Acta Materialia Inc. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Acta Materialia Inc. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Alyami, Mohammed
Mahmoud, Nada T
Habib, Rached
El-Bashir, Samah
coredata.dc:description:
Green fluorescing PMMA-InP/ZnS nanohybrid coating films were applied onto FTO glass substrates through the traditional spin coating technique for the development of luminescent solar concentrator windows. Examination using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) displayed good morphology and uniform dispersion of InP/ZnS quantum dots (QDs) in PMMA matrix, whereas X-ray diffraction (XRD) indicated the amorphous nature and good adhesion to FTO glass substrate. Investigations of the influence of varying QD concentrations on spectral photophysical characteristics were conducted using techniques such as optical absorption, transmission, fluorescence spectroscopy, and the chromaticity diagram (CIE 1931). The films exhibited good transparency in the visible spectrum, approximately 87 %, with reduced transparency in the near-infrared (NIR) region, approximately 26 %. The films have various shades of green colors closely aligned with the sensitivity of the human eye, as demonstrated by CIE 1931 chromaticity diagram. Skin depth calculations spanning the UV to NIR wavelengths (200-2400 nm) showed a marked decrease in electromagnetic wave penetration correlating with increased InP-ZnS QD concentrations. Fluorescence spectroscopy revealed the highest fluorescence intensity and quantum yield of 71 % for QDs concentration of
0.15
w t %
coupled with outstanding photostability properties after exposure to UVA radiation (365 nm) for 72 h. This optimization effectively reduces the infiltration of detrimental solar radiation into buildings, encompassing ultraviolet and infrared wavelengths. The research outcomes are consistent with the objectives of Sustainable Development Goals SDG7 and SDG 11, in sun-drenched regions such as Saudi Arabia, as PMMA-InP/ZnS nanohybrid coating films can be tailored to meet the spectral needs of energy-efficient windows.
coredata.dcterms:subject: PMMA- InP/ZnS Nanohybrids
FTO glass
IR Blocking
Sustainable Buildings

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The working principle of PMMA-InP/ZnS nanohybrid film coated on FTO glass for LSC energy-efficient window applications.
PMMA-InP/ZnS nanohybrid film coated on FTO glass under 365 nm UV illumination.
TEM micrograph of PMMA nanohybrid film doped with 0.15 wt% InP/ZnS QDs.
XRD diffraction patterns for uncoated FTO glass and FTO glass coated with PMMA- InP/ZnS nanohybrid films containing different concentrations of InP/ZnS QDs.
Absorption spectra of PMMA-InP/ZnS nanohybrid films coated on FTO glass.
Transmission spectra for PMMA-InP/ZnS nanohybrid films coated on FTO glass, the visible range is magnified inset.
The spectral dependence of the skin depth, δ, for PMMA-InP/ZnS nanohybrid films coated on FTO glass.
Tauc Plots for PMMA-InP/ZnS nanohybrid films coated on FTO glass.
Fluorescence spectra of PMMA-InP/ZnS nanohybrid films coated on FTO glass.
CIE 1931 chromaticity diagram of PMMA-InP/ZnS nanohybrid films coated on FTO glass.
Photodegradation curves of PMMA-InP/ZnS nanohybrid films coated on FTO glass after exposure to UVA light (365 nm) for 72 h.
A representative diagram of fluorescence quenching for PMMA doped with 0.15 wt% InP/ZnS nanohybrid films coated on FTO glass directly exposed to UVA light (365 nm) for 72 h.
The optical energy band gap energy, Eg , transmission, T %, fluorescence quantum yield ΦF , the absorption, emission maxima λa and λf for PMMA/InP-ZnS nanohybrid films coated on FTO glass.
Photodegradation parameters for PMMA/InP-ZnS nanohybrid films coated on FTO glass after irradiation with UVA light (365 nm) for 72 h.

head.ce:title: New luminescent solar concentrator windows using PMMA-InP/ZnS Nanohybrid coating films for green building applications

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Green fluorescing PMMA-InP/ZnS nanohybrid coating films were applied onto FTO glass substrates through the traditional spin coating technique for the development of luminescent solar concentrator windows. Examination using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) displayed good morphology and uniform dispersion of InP/ZnS quantum dots (QDs) in PMMA matrix, whereas X-ray diffraction (XRD) indicated the amorphous nature and good adhesion to FTO glass substrate. Investigations of the influence of varying QD concentrations on spectral photophysical characteristics were conducted using techniques such as optical absorption, transmission, fluorescence spectroscopy, and the chromaticity diagram (CIE 1931). The films exhibited good transparency in the visible spectrum, approximately 87 %, with reduced transparency in the near-infrared (NIR) region, approximately 26 %. The films have various shades of green colors closely aligned with the sensitivity of the human eye, as demonstrated by CIE 1931 chromaticity diagram. Skin depth calculations spanning the UV to NIR wavelengths (200-2400 nm) showed a marked decrease in electromagnetic wave penetration correlating with increased InP-ZnS QD concentrations. Fluorescence spectroscopy revealed the highest fluorescence intensity and quantum yield of 71 % for QDs concentration of
0.15
w t %
coupled with outstanding photostability properties after exposure to UVA radiation (365 nm) for 72 h. This optimization effectively reduces the infiltration of detrimental solar radiation into buildings, encompassing ultraviolet and infrared wavelengths. The research outcomes are consistent with the objectives of Sustainable Development Goals SDG7 and SDG 11, in sun-drenched regions such as Saudi Arabia, as PMMA-InP/ZnS nanohybrid coating films can be tailored to meet the spectral needs of energy-efficient windows.

ce:section.ce:para: Energy-efficient window glazing technologies represent an important strategy to reduce the global carbon footprint and enhance the energy performance of sustainable buildings [1,2]. Energy-efficient windows often utilize different types of coating materials that have their advantages and disadvantages, with the best choice depending on several factors such as climate, building orientation, energy efficiency goals, and economic considerations [3,4]. Various types of coatings frequently used are Low-Emissivity (Low-E), tinted, reflective, spectrally selective, thermochromic, and electrochromic coatings [5]. Luminescent Solar Concentrators (LSCs) have become a promising technology that can turn windows into energy-efficient components in building design including energy harvesting [6], energy efficiency [7], smart control [8,9], and ambient light utilization [10]. Moreover, LSCs have a potential environmental impact on reducing the carbon footprint of the building sector which accounts for approximately 28 % of global CO2 Emissions [11]. Luminescent solar concentrators (LSCs) function through the utilization of light-emitting substances, such as luminescent dyes or quantum dots doped in a transparent host matrix with a high refractive index [12]. The emitted wavelengths are then trapped within LSC and guided by total internal reflection toward the edges where they are absorbed by spectrally matched PV cells [13,14]. LSCs can concentrate this light onto small, high-efficiency PV cells located at the edges, thus reducing the usage of photovoltaics and the overall cost [15]. One of the significant advantages of LSCs is their potential for use in building-integrated photovoltaics (BIPV) because they can be incorporated into windows, façades, or other structures without significantly altering their appearance [16–18]. In terms of energy harvesting, LSCs are capable of collecting both direct and diffuse sunlight, making them adaptable to a range of lighting environments [19]. These features make them a promising technology for increasing the efficiency of solar energy harvesting in urban environments where space is limited [20,21]. The host matrix plays a crucial role in the performance of LSCs and must meet various requirements [12] such as optical properties, chemical compatibility with dopant luminophores, processability, and weathering durability. Traditionally, polymeric systems (e.g., polyacrylates, polycarbonates) have been used as host matrices for LSCs due to their high refractive index, easy fabrication, low cost, and long-lasting performance [22–24]. Researchers have investigated new host matrix materials in advancements to enhance the efficiency and durability of LSCs Scientists have explored novel host matrix materials such as organic-inorganic nanohybrids [25], renewable polyesters [26], cellulose nanocrystals (CNCs) [27], and thermoresponsive polymers [28]. Recently, the application of luminescent coatings on fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) glass has emerged as a novel method for improving solar radiation filtering for window applications [29]. The electrical conduction of FTO glass and the light-managing capabilities of luminescent materials have created efficient and versatile solar energy solutions including photocatalysis, hydrogen production [30–32], and semitransparent solar cells [33–35], while also offering UV and IR-blocking capabilities for spectrally selective window purposes [4,29,36–38]. Regarding luminescent dopant materials, InP quantum dots (QDs) have emerged as a potential alternative to cadmium-based QDs due to the reduced toxicity, excellent stability and tunable emission properties for various applications such as optoelectronic devices [39], light emitting diodes (LEDs) [40], green chemistry and energy conversion [41] and luminescent solar concentrators [42–45].

The current study presented a new LSC window based on polymethylmethacrylate (PMMA) film doped with InP/ZnS quantum dots (QDs) coated on FTO glass substrates. The films have outstanding photostability and tuned spectral photophysical properties that can filter out a large part of solar ultraviolet and infrared and allow visible light to pass through as illustrated in Fig. 1 . The proposed LSC windows are highly transparent and spectrally selective, providing specialized control over solar energy entry into buildings. They modify the optical properties of window glazing to enhance energy efficiency by minimizing solar heat gain and glare, all while maintaining ample natural daylight. Such a feature is crucial for lowering cooling expenses in building environments and advancing sustainability goals, particularly under the climatic conditions of Saudi Arabia.

InP/ZnS core-shell quantum dots (QDs) were supplied from Sigma-Aldrich (USA) as a toluene suspension at a concentration of 5 mg/mL. PMMA powder, with an average molecular weight of around 15,000 g/mol, was supplied by SABIC (KSA). High-purity toluene (HPLC grade) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (USA). Fluorine-doped oxide (FTO) coated glass, 2 mm thick, was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich, USA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of PMMA-InP/ZnS nanohybrid coating films
ce:section.ce:para: PMMA/toluene solution was prepared by mixing 5 g of PMMA with 100 ml of toluene, the mixture was then stirred in a sealed beaker at 40 °C using a magnetic stirrer for 2 hours. Subsequently, InP/ZnS quantum dots (QDs) were added to the solution at concentrations ranging from 0.05 to 0.25 wt%, respectively. Subsequently, PMMA-InP/ZnS nanohybrid coating films were deposited by pouring the prepared solutions onto FTO glass substrates at an angular velocity 2000 rpm to achieve uniform film thicknesses Subsequently, the films were air-dried at ambient temperature and further aged at 50°C for 6 hours to ensure full evaporation of the solvent. The film thickness was measured utilizing the Fizeau interferometer technique, resulting in an average thickness of around 250 ± 5 nm.

The morphology and dopant distribution of PMMA-InP/ZnS nanohybrid coatings on FTO glass were examined using a transmission electron microscope (TEM) system (JEOL JEM-1400, Japan). X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was studied in the range 10o ≤ 2θ ≤ 80 o utilizing a diffractometer (BRUKER, D8 ADVANCE, Germany). The optical absorbance and transmittance spectra were recorded at room temperature across the wavelength range (200-2400 nm)using a spectrophotometer (JASCO, V-770, UV-VIS-NIR, Japan); the software integrated into the instrument for computing the chromaticity coordinates. The steady-state fluorescence spectra were recorded at room temperature within the wavelength range (400-700 nm) using a spectrofluorometer model (SHIMADZU, RF-6000 Spectrofluorophotometer, Japan). The photostability of the samples was evaluated in the laboratory by monitoring the relative changes in the optical density and fluorescence intensity under direct exposure to UVA irradiation (365 nm) from a UV lamp (6 W, 115 VAC/60 Hz, Cole Parmer, USA) for 72 h, as shown in Fig. 2 .

The morphology and distribution of InP/ZnS QDs in the polymer matrix has been investigated by TEM for the doping concentration 0.15 wt% as shown by the micrograph depicted in Fig. 3 . The image illustrates the good dispersion and uniform distribution of spherical-shaped InP/ZnS nanocrystal dopant. This study also showed that the majority of InP/ZnS QDs have a size of 3.5 nm with a few exceptions as some of them have increased size ranging from 4 to 6 nm due to nanocrystal aggregation after being doped in the dense viscous media PMMA matrix.

XRD patterns of PMMA-InP/ZnS nanohybrid films applied onto FTO glass were analyzed within the scanning range 10o ≤ 2θ ≤ 80o, as depicted in the representative diagram in Fig. 4 , comparing the uncoated FTO glass with FTO glass coated with a PMMA nanohybrid film containing 0.25 wt% InP/ZnS QDs. The structure of the prepared films, amorphous or semicrystalline, is not evident as no distinctive peaks can be observed. In previous studies, XRD spectra of the PMMA coated on both FTO and ITO conductive glass substrates typically demonstrated an amorphous nature at room temperature [46]. According to JCPDS card No. 041-1445, SnO2 has a tetragonal structure recognized by eight distinguished peaks related to Miller planes (110), (101), (200), (211), (220),(310),(301) and (321). After matching, only five dominant orientation peaks of SnO2 have appeared in Miller indices (110), (200), (211), (310) and (301). The decrease in the peak intensity after coating FTO with PMMA - InP/ZnS nanohybrid films can be attributed to the strong amorphous nature of the PMMA matrix, which masked the most of distinct diffraction peaks of the crystalline materials [47], this behavior reflects the good adhesion between the nanohybrid coating film and FTO glass substrate. The type of chemical bonding between PMMA - InP/ZnS nanohybrid coating films and the surface of FTO conductive glass can involve various interactions, including van der Waals forces, electrostatic interactions, and possibly chemical bonding [48,49].

Investigating the optical properties of transparent polymer films holds significant technological value. The absorption spectra of PMMA-InP/ZnS nanohybrid films coated on FTO glass are illustrated in Fig. 5 . In the wavelength range (200-2400 nm), a redshift is observed in the absorption edge of the films in the ultraviolet spectrum, as well as in the visible spectrum. The observed redshift in the absorption edge of PMMA after doping with InP/ZnS QDs can be a result of the interaction between the dopant QDs and the polymer matrix [47]. This interaction alters the electronic structure of PMMA, leading to the complex formation between the embedded dopant molecules within the polymer matrix. Previous studies reported that luminescent dopant molecules redistribute the electron density and modify the energy levels within PMMA host matrix [50]. Furthermore, the presence of a redshift indicates a decreased band gap in the doped PMMA films, allowing for the absorption of visible light at extended wavelengths in comparison to undoped transparent PMMA [51]. The high absorption observed between 1200-2400 nm in the PMMA-InP/ZnS nanohybrid films coated on FTO glass substrates may result from the inherent material properties and specific cut-off characteristics influencing the absorption spectrum in this range. In the literature, it has been reported that FTO glass substrates are known for their high transparency in the visible range but show distinct absorption features in the NIR, possibly due to defects or specific doping characteristics of the tin oxide layer [52]. The absorption maximum values, λa, are recorded in Table 1 , this characteristic wavelength is essential for applications that require accurate absorption and emission of specific wavelengths, especially in areas such as gas sensors [53,54], luminescent solar concentrators [55], laser technology [56,57], and optoelectronic devices [58]. Fig. 6 illustrates the transmission spectra of PMMA-InP/ZnS nanohybrid films deposited on FTO glass, the average transmittance value for each sample is listed in Table 1. It is observed that the films demonstrate high transmittance in the visible spectrum,
(
≅ 87
░ %
)
, while exhibiting lower transmittance in the near infrared (NIR) region around
(
≅ 26
░ %
)
. The optimization of light transmittance in PMMA-InP/ZnS nanohybrids plays a critical role in energy-related applications such as solar panels and smart windows. This feature guarantees efficient energy absorption or light transmission, improving overall performance and efficiency. Fundamentally, maximizing light transmittance enhances energy utilization and develops sustainable energy solutions in a wide range of applications.

The skin depth, denoted as δ, is defined as the distance at which the electromagnetic field intensity diminishes to approximately 37 % (1/e) of its initial surface level within a conductive substance [59]. In the case here of transparent dielectric materials such as PMMA, the skin depth relates to the depth at which electromagnetic radiation diminishes due to absorption [60]. Hence, the skin depth can be determined based on the absorption coefficient (α) through the following relation [61],
(1)
δ = 1 / α

Where the optical absorption coefficient,α , was calculated from Beer's law from the transmittance, T, using the following formula [62,63],
(2)
∝ = − ln T / d

Fig. 7 demonstrates how the skin depth varies across the UV to NIR range (200-2400 nm) of the electromagnetic spectrum, as the concentration of InP/ZnS QDs increases, the skin depth decreases. Skin depth is a significant factor in optically transparent materials, as it indicates the depth to which electromagnetic waves can penetrate the material before experiencing a notable reduction in intensity. This parameter has critical implications across a range of applications such as electromagnetic shielding, wireless communication, dielectric coatings, and waveguides. Furthermore, in the fields of Safety and Medicine, understanding skin depth is essential for the precise delivery of radiation doses during medical treatments, guaranteeing efficacy while protecting surrounding healthy tissues. Hence, the significance of skin depth highlights its vital contribution to progress in the fields of technology, science, and healthcare.

Tauc's plot, which is commonly used in the analysis of optical absorption data, offers insights into the electronic structure of materials, particularly semiconductors and insulators. Fig. 8 represents a graphical presentation of Tauc plots for PMMA-InP/ZnS nanohybrid films which revealed direct allowed transition according to Tauc's relation between the absorption coefficient, α , and photon energy E,
(3)
α E = B
(
E −
E g
)
m

Here B is a constant, and Eg represents the optical energy bandgap, which can either be a direct allowed transition at m=½ or an indirect allowed transition at m=2. These plots are crucial for understanding the bandgap characteristics and optical properties of various types of PMMA films. By analyzing Tauc plots for PMMA-InP/ZnS nanohybrid films, the optimal least squares fit was achieved at m=½, indicating a direct electronic transition through the allowed band gap. The information illustrated in Table 1 shows a reduction in the bandgap energy values, Eg, this effect becomes more noticeable at high doping concentrations as the bandgap energy decreases from 4.96 eV for pure PMMA to 4.22 eV for 0.25 wt% doping concentration of InP/ZnS QDs. This reduction in the band gap with increased doping concentration can be attributed to the creation of new energy states within the band gap energy due to the interaction between the dopant and the host material. Typically, the decrease in the band gap energy can be attributed to greater degree of structural disorder increased by increasing the concentration of InP/ZnS QDs. For example, the dopant molecules lower the energy of the PMMA lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO), causing the absorption edge to shift to lower photon energies and subsequently a decreased Eg . This behavior indicates that the introduction of InP/ZnS QDs can adjust the electronic structure of PMMA matrix. This adjustment has the potential to improve the optical characteristics of the material, potentially rendering it more appropriate for uses requiring specific light absorption or emission properties. It was demonstrated that the reduction in Eg values due to doping is essential for tailoring material properties to suit various technological applications, this is because of the modification of the band structure which is affected by the type of the host polymer matrix and the type of dopant materials.

The Fluorescence spectra of PMMA-InP/ZnS nanohybrid films coated on FTO glass are displayed in Fig. 9 spanning the wavelength range of 400-700 nm. Remarkably, all the films exhibit a symmetrical emission peak, as evidenced by the fluorescence maxima recorded around 535 nm for each film in Table 1. Quantum dots are commonly recognized for displaying clearly defined, symmetrical emission peaks that correspond to their discrete energy levels which are influenced by the size and composition of the quantum dots. Modifying the size of quantum dots during the manufacturing process allows for precise control over the shape and location of their emission peaks. This unique characteristic enables the customization of quantum dots to meet specific requirements in photonic, imaging, and optoelectronic applications. On the other hand, the fluorescence intensity of PMMA-InP/ZnS nanohybrid films fluctuates by increasing the concentration of QDs, displaying a maximum at a doping level of 0.15 wt% before it becomes quenched. The radiative efficiency of a fluorescent sample can be measured by fluorescence quantum yield,
Φ
f , s
,
which is determined by the percentage of absorbed photons converted as fluorescence, taking the subscripts “r” and “s” for the reference and the sample

Φ
f , s
can be calculated using [64–66],
(4)
Φ
f , s
=
Φ
f , r
M r
M s
A s
A r
n s 2
n r 2

Where
Φ
f , r
denotes the fluorescence quantum yield of the reference (79 % for Fluorescein 1M in NaOH). The areas under the fluorescence curves are denoted by Mr ​ and Ms in cm-1. The optical densities are represented by Ar
and As ​, while the refractive indices are indicated as nr and ns . The information provided in Table 1 indicates a direct relationship between concentration and fluorescence quantum yield,
Φ
f , s ,
mirroring the pattern seen in fluorescence intensity. With an increase in doping concentration, the fluorescence quantum yield of green-emitting PMMA-InP/ZnS nanohybrid films rises, peaking at 71 % when reaching 0.15wt %. After surpassing this emission threshold, the quantum yield decreases, the presence of quantum dots in the polymer matrix impacts the fluorescence quantum yield in two manners. Initially, with concentrations up to 0.15 wt%, the rise in the absorption coefficient results in a higher number of emitted photons. However, with concentrations exceeding 0.15 wt%, the probability of fluorescence quenching increases due to the increased spectral overlap (small Stoke's shift) resulting from aggregate formation [67–69]. Consequently, the excited fluorophores dissipate their energy to other entities or the host PMMA matrix instead of emitting a photon, thus reducing the quantum yield,
Φ
f , s ,
at large doping concentrations [70–72]. These results indicate that an efficient excitation of QDs occurs at concentrations
≥ 0.15
░ wt %
resulting
in a higher emission intensity. Therefore, understanding and controlling QD concentration in nanohybrid films are crucial for optimizing their fluorescence properties and ensuring their applicability in various optical and sensing devices. However, exceeding this ideal concentration level results in a reduction in fluorescence intensity, suggesting a quenching phenomenon. This quenching effect may stem from different factors like QD aggregation, non-radiative energy transfer mechanisms, or reabsorption phenomena, all of which can diminish the emitted fluorescence.

The color gamut of PMMA-InP/ZnS nanohybrid films coated on FTO glass is represented by CIE 1931 color space shown in Fig. 10 . These films display various shades of viable green that closely align with human eye sensitivity, reflected by their hue, saturation, and brightness coordinates. Initially, at lower concentrations of InP/ZnS QDs, the green emission appears confined to a limited area on the CIE 1931 chromaticity diagram, indicating restricted hues and saturations. However, with increased quantum dot concentration, the emitted light expands across a broader spectrum within the diagram, offering a wider array of colors. The shape and size of the color gamut within CIE 1931 color space are influenced by factors including emission spectra, size, shape, concentration, and any spectral overlap between absorption and fluorescence spectra. These films can reduce solar heat inside buildings by selectively filtering incoming sunlight, controlling glares, minimizing excessive brightness, and improving energy efficiency by managing light transmission. The adjustable color range is crucial for achieving visual appeal and functional effectiveness in building glazing applications in Saudi Arabia.

The photodegradation, P, has been calculated as the relative change in the optical density of InP/ZnS QDs after exposure to electromagnetic radiation for a specific time “t”. Fig. 11 illustrates the photodegradation curves of PMMA-InP/ZnS nanohybrid films coated on FTO glass irradiated with UVA (365 nm) for 72 h. All the films exhibit one degradation step according to the following exponential decay relation [24];
(5)
P =
P o
e
− R t
+
A t
A o

Where Po is the fitting constant, “R” is the photodegradation rate constant, and At/Ao designates the relative change in the optical density for the remainder of the dopant InP/ZnS QDs after exposing the nanohybrid films to UVA radiation. The photodegradation parameters listed in Table 2 , it is evident that the photodegradation rate constant is decreased by increasing the concentration of InP/ZnS QDs and the dopant remainder is increased. The decreasing rate of photodegradation of quantum dots (QDs) with increasing concentration can be attributed to different factors [73–75] such as quantum dot aggregation, increased carrier recombination rate, and light scattering. It has been reported that the aggregation of QDs can lead to adecrease in the surface area available for the photodegradation reactions [76–78]. Additionally, increased recombination reduces the availability of charge carriers for photodegradation, while the higher light scattering hinders the penetration of light through PMMA host matrix to reach all quantum dots. These factors combined can contribute to the observed decrease in the rate of photodegradation of PMMA-ZnS/InP nanohybrid films as the concentration of QDs increases.

On the other hand, the fluorescence spectra of all the films showed emission quenching upon exposure to UVA radiation, without any changes in peak shape or position as revealed by the representative plot represented in Fig. 12 . The quantum yield of emission quenching upon exposure to UVA,
Φ
f , s
(
U V A
)
, was calculated using Eq. (4) and listed in Table 2. The doping concentration
0.15
░ wt %
showed the lowest reduction in fluorescence efficiency (81 %) after being exposed to UVA radiation for 72 hours. This can be attributed to the optimal radiative characteristics at this concentration, which reduces the formation of oxidized layers that function as trapping states that cause structural defects in QDs and affect their emission properties [79].

In conclusion, the proper choice of the host matrix is a crucial factor for energy-efficient LSC window applications to optimize energy harvesting, energy efficiency, and photostability. The present research showed that PMMA-InP/ZnS nanohybrid films coated on FTO glass have provided UV and IR-blocking features for LSC window applications. The samples have a tuned symmetrical green emission peak at approximately 535 nm, resulting in the highest fluorescence efficiency of 71 % for the doping concentration of 0.15 wt% InP/ZnS QDs. This optimal doping concentration helped to maintain the structural integrity and radiative characteristics of InP/ZnS QDs within the host PMMA matrix, thereby reducing the rate of photodegradation and preserving fluorescence efficiency upon prolonged UVA exposure. The range of green colors exhibited by the LSC window samples, as depicted in the CIE 1931 color space, closely resembles the perception of the human eye. This alignment carries significant implications for the development of energy-efficient LSC windows. The proposed LSC window samples showed excellent transmittance in the visible range of approximately 87 %, while displaying reduced transmittance in the near-infrared (NIR) range at around 26 %. By selectively filtering incoming sunlight and controlling light transmission, these technologies can reduce solar heat inside buildings and reduce the consumption of lighting and air conditioning. The obtained results align with the initiatives of Saudi Arabia to achieve sustainable development goals, particularly SDG 7 (Affordable and Clean Energy) and SDG 11 (Sustainable Cities and Communities), by promoting energy efficiency and environmentally friendly urban growth strategies.
This project is sponsored by Prince Sattam Bin Abdulaziz University (PSAU) as part of funding for its SDG Roadmap Research Funding Program project number PSAU/2023/SDG/36.

Mohammed Alyami: Writing – original draft, Validation, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Data curation. Nada T Mahmoud: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Data curation. Rached Habib: Writing – review & editing, Investigation, Data curation. Samah El-Bashir: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.mtla.2024.102292.
